af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Excellent multi-language SDK coverage (Python, Ruby, PHP, Java, JS, .NET, Go). Simple API key passed as Basic Auth username — clean for agents. Test and production keys are separate (prevents accidental label purchases). Rate shopping is async — agents must compare rates before purchasing. Webhook signature verification available. Carrier-specific errors require per-carrier handling. Address verification before shipment creation is best practice.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. API key as Basic Auth — no scope granularity. Test/production key separation prevents accidental label creation. SOC2 compliance. API key via environment variable. Multi-language SDK maintained by EasyPost. No per-operation or per-carrier permission scoping — full account access on every key. Webhook HMAC signature verification available for production security.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: SaaS with SLA and status page. Stable versioned API across multi-language SDKs. Good migration guides. Retry guidance documented. Label creation is not idempotent — retrying may create duplicate labels and charges. Rate shopping results are ephemeral — rates must be re-fetched after expiry. Carrier-specific downtime affects available carriers in real-time.

af_score_components.notes: Local MCP server for ebook reading/conversion (quality 62). No auth required — zero friction. Supports EPUB, PDF, and common ebook formats. No rate limits (local tool). Limited tool surface — basic read/convert operations. Minimal documentation beyond README. Error handling for corrupted or DRM-protected files is limited. Useful niche tool for document processing workflows.

security_score_components.security_notes: Local MCP server with no network exposure — minimal attack surface. No credentials needed or stored. Read-only ebook operations reduce risk. Unknown version and dependency audit status. macOS local access controls provide implicit security boundary. DRM-protected content handling may have legal considerations depending on jurisdiction.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Self-hosted local tool with no SLA. Unknown version — API surface may change. No documented breaking change policy. Read operations are idempotent. Reliability depends on local machine uptime. File format support limited to supported ebook types — unsupported formats fail silently.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Good documentation with OpenAPI spec. API key auth is clean for agents. Manages Elastic Cloud deployments (create, scale, delete) — not data plane search operations. Deployment IDs are required for most operations — agents must discover or store them. Operations are async — agents must poll deployment status. Rate limits depend on plan tier. Separate from Elasticsearch data API — different auth surface.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. API key auth — limited scope granularity for control plane operations. Elastic-maintained infrastructure. SOC2/ISO27001/GDPR compliance for cloud management plane. API key via environment variable. Deployment-level operations have high blast radius — agents can terminate clusters. Consider read-only keys for monitoring-only workflows.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Managed cloud with SLA and status page. Stable versioned API. Migration guides available. Retry guidance documented. Async deployment operations require polling with appropriate timeouts. Idempotent GET operations safe to retry. POST operations (create deployments) may create duplicates — check existence before creating.

af_score_components.notes: Official Elastic MCP server (quality 78). Excellent documentation — one of the most comprehensive in the ecosystem. Multiple auth methods — API key recommended for agents. Fine-grained RBAC enables least-privilege agent access. Index mapping must be understood before querying — schema discovery required. Query DSL is complex but powerful. Scroll API for large result sets. Cloud and self-hosted have different auth/rate limit behavior. Version differences (7.x vs 8.x) affect API shape.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced for Elastic Cloud; self-hosted requires TLS configuration. Multiple auth methods: API key (preferred), basic auth, bearer token, PKI cert. Fine-grained RBAC with per-index and per-field access control. SOC2/ISO27001/GDPR on Cloud. API keys with limited privileges via environment variables. Self-hosted security is operator's responsibility. Excellent security architecture for a data platform.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Elastic Cloud has SLA; self-hosted operator-managed. Stable versioned API — 7.x and 8.x maintained in parallel. Good breaking change management with migration guides. Retry guidance documented. Cursor-based (scroll/pit) pagination for large datasets. GET queries idempotent. Write operations not idempotent — use external IDs for document deduplication. Cluster health must be green for writes.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Good documentation with multi-language SDK. Simple API key. Voice generation is stateful — must select voice_id before generating. Custom voices require prior upload. Generated audio is not stored server-side beyond short TTL — must download immediately. Streaming generation available for real-time use cases. Character limits are per-request, not just monthly. Latency optimization settings for streaming vs quality.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. Simple API key — no scope granularity. ElevenLabs maintained. No formal compliance certifications listed for consumer audio platform. API key via environment variable. Voice cloning with uploaded audio raises consent and misuse concerns — must follow ElevenLabs ToS. Generated audio attribution and watermarking may apply depending on plan.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: SaaS with status page. Relatively stable API versioning. Migration guides available for major changes. Retry guidance documented. Audio generation not idempotent — same text may produce slightly different output. Character credits consumed even on error in some cases. Streaming endpoint has different reliability characteristics than batch generation. Plan-based rate limits require monitoring.

af_score_components.notes: MCP server with low quality (38). Chinese enterprise information scanner — scrapes corporate registry data, investment relationships, and personnel info from Chinese business databases. Browser cookie auth is complex and brittle for agents. Documentation primarily in Chinese. Rate limits from upstream sources are opaque and unpredictable. Legal and compliance concerns for automated corporate intelligence gathering. Scraping-based — may break without notice when upstream sites change.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS for scraped sources. Browser cookie auth is weak and session-dependent — poor for agents. No scope granularity. Unknown version and dependency audit status. Browser cookies stored in environment variables is fragile. Corporate intelligence data raises data protection and legal compliance concerns in some jurisdictions. No formal compliance certifications.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Self-hosted with no SLA. Unknown version — scraping layer fragile to upstream site changes. No documented breaking change policy. No idempotency guarantees on scraping operations. Rate limits from upstream sources can cause silent failures. High maintenance burden to keep cookie sessions alive. Not suitable for production agent workflows requiring consistent data access.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. SMART on FHIR OAuth2 is the most complex auth pattern for agents — requires per-patient or system-level authorization flows. FHIR R4 standard provides structured clinical data. Each Epic instance may have different endpoint URLs. System-level JWT auth available for backend agents (no user interaction). Rate limits vary per Epic instance and not publicly documented. FHIR resource versioning (R4 vs STU3) requires explicit handling. Mandatory HIPAA compliance for any agent accessing PHI.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. SMART on FHIR OAuth2 with granular clinical scopes (patient/Observation.read, system/Patient.read, etc.). HIPAA BAA required for any PHI access — gold-standard compliance for healthcare. Epic-maintained infrastructure. OAuth2 tokens via secure flow. Scope granularity enables least-privilege access per clinical resource type. System JWT auth for backend agents uses RSA key pairs — require HSM or secrets manager.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Enterprise healthcare SaaS with high uptime expectations. FHIR standard provides API stability across Epic versions. FHIR versioning (R4, STU3) managed with migration guides. Retry guidance in FHIR spec. Partial idempotency — GET operations safe to retry. POST operations (write clinical data) require external idempotency keys. Rate limits per instance require pre-negotiation. Epic MyChart OAuth flows add user interaction dependencies.

af_score_components.notes: MCP server with low quality (45). Controls ESP32-based NAT router firmware — niche IoT/embedded use case. No rate limits (local device). Password auth is simple. Limited tool coverage — basic network config operations (SSID, password, routing). Documentation minimal — primarily README. Local-only operation with no cloud dependency. Useful for home lab automation but not production networking. Device must be accessible on local network.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTP on local network — TLS typically not configured on ESP32 firmware by default. Password auth only — no key rotation or scope control. Device password stored in agent config. No formal security hardening for IoT firmware. Network configuration changes can disrupt connectivity. No compliance certifications. High blast radius if agent misconfigures routing table. Not suitable for production network infrastructure.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Self-hosted IoT device with no SLA. Firmware version-dependent behavior. No documented breaking change policy. Network operations not idempotent — config changes take effect immediately. Device reboot required for some config changes. Local network availability required. Device hardware failure means no recovery path. Not suitable for mission-critical network management.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Good blockchain data API documentation. Simple API key. Free tier is very restrictive (5 calls/sec, 100k/day) — production agents need paid plan. All reads are idempotent — blockchain data is immutable once confirmed. Transaction data requires confirmations before considered final — agents must wait for block confirmations. Blockchain-specific concepts (gas, blocks, ERC20 vs ERC721) require domain knowledge. Multichain support (Etherscan, Polygonscan, BSCScan) uses same API pattern.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. Simple API key — no scope granularity. Read-only blockchain data queries — no write operations via Etherscan (blockchain writes go directly to node). Etherscan maintained. API key via environment variable. No compliance certifications for blockchain data platform. Rate limits protect against abuse but restrictive on free tier. Data is public blockchain data — no PII concerns.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: SaaS with status page. Stable API versioning. Good backward compatibility history. Retry guidance documented. Full GET idempotency on all blockchain data queries. Free tier rate limits (5/sec) can cause silent failures in rapid querying agents. Blockchain confirmation delays are an inherent reliability consideration — unconfirmed transactions may revert. Chain reorganizations can change historical data in edge cases.

af_score_components.notes: Community MCP server for EVM blockchain interaction (quality 62). Supports reading chain data and potentially executing transactions via configured wallet. Private key management via environment variables is a significant security concern for transaction signing. RPC provider must be configured separately. No rate limits on read operations but RPC provider limits apply. Blockchain transaction failures (gas estimation, reverts) require specialized error handling. Read operations safe; write operations (transactions) are irreversible. Early-stage tool with limited error guidance.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS to RPC providers. CRITICAL: Private key in environment variable is dangerous — compromise leads to permanent asset loss. No scope granularity — full wallet access if private key provided. Unknown version and dependency audit status. No compliance certifications. Private keys should use hardware wallets or dedicated signing services, not env vars. Transaction signing capability makes this extremely high-risk if misconfigured. Read-only mode (no private key) is much safer for most agent use cases.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Self-hosted with no SLA — depends on RPC provider availability. Unknown version — API surface may change. No documented breaking change policy. Read operations idempotent; transactions irreversible. Gas estimation failures cause transactions to revert — agents must handle this. RPC provider rate limits apply. Blockchain network congestion affects transaction inclusion times unpredictably. High-risk tool requiring careful operational controls.
